explanation: Enteropathogenic E. coli efface the microvilli and induce localized actin polymerization, forming characteristic actin pedestals beneath the attached bacteria, disrupting absorption.
The polar filament is a flagellum, a protein cylinder anchored in the cell envelope that rotates to propel the bacterium; adhesion is a role of pili/fimbriae, and conjugative transfer uses sex pili.
The absence of a nucleus with the nucleoid/circular DNA free in the cytoplasm, plus a capsule, cell wall, pili and flagellum, identifies this as a prokaryotic bacterial cell.
Cord factor (trehalose dimycolate) causes M. tuberculosis to grow in the characteristic serpentine cords seen here and is toxic to mammalian cells while inhibiting neutrophil migration.
Chlamydia species replicate inside a membrane-bound endosome (inclusion) within the host-cell cytoplasm, which enlarges as the bacteria multiply and can eventually dominate the cell volume.
At the end of the chlamydial cycle, reticulate bodies convert back to elementary bodies and the infected cell lyses, releasing infectious elementary bodies that go on to infect neighboring cells.

Legionella pneumophila is a facultative intracellular pathogen taken up by macrophages via coiling phagocytosis; it then inhibits phagosome-lysosome fusion, allowing it to persist and replicate inside the phagocyte.
CMV massively enlarges infected cells (cyto = cell, megalo = large) and produces a large basophilic intranuclear inclusion with a surrounding clear halo, the classic 'owl's eye' inclusion.

brick: Normal Flora: Role in Health and Disease
explanation: A vaginal epithelial cell coated with adherent bacteria (obscuring its borders) is a clue cell, the microscopic hallmark of bacterial vaginosis, typically due to Gardnerella vaginalis overgrowth.

brick: Normal Flora: Role in Health and Disease
explanation: In young children the eustachian tube is shorter, narrower, and more horizontal, allowing nasopharyngeal flora to migrate into the middle ear and impairing drainage, which predisposes to acute otitis media.
Giant thick-walled spherules filled with endospores are the diagnostic tissue form of Coccidioides.
Dermatophytes feed on keratin found only in the stratum corneum, hair, and nails, so they remain limited to keratinized superficial layers and rarely cause deep or systemic disease.
Cestodes (tapeworms) have a scolex (head) and a chain of segments called proglottids; each mature proglottid is hermaphroditic, containing both male and female organs.
Adult schistosomes (blood flukes) are unusual among trematodes in having separate sexes; the male's gynecophoral canal holds the female during continuous mating.
N. fowleri uniquely exists as trophozoite, transient flagellate, and cyst forms; it causes primary amebic meningoencephalitis.
T. gondii persists as bradyzoite-filled tissue cysts that reactivate when cell-mediated immunity wanes.
Female phlebotomine sandflies transmit Leishmania spp.; mosquitoes transmit malaria and reduviid bugs transmit Chagas disease.

Hookworms (Ancylostoma duodenale shown; Necator americanus uses cutting plates) use these buccal teeth/plates to grip and lacerate the small-bowel mucosa, feeding on blood and causing iron-deficiency anemia in heavy infections.
These are Charcot-Leyden crystals, formed from galectin-10 released by degranulating eosinophils; they signal eosinophil-rich processes such as parasitic or allergic disease.

Gram-positive cocci that bunch into grape-like clusters are characteristic of Staphylococcus, which divides in multiple planes. Streptococci form chains, Neisseria form diplococci, and Bacillus/Clostridium are rods.
